author: @The_Alpha_E
text:
''fake'' is the wrong word.

Being ''fake'' implies a level of deceit. Think of a fake Rolex watch, for example. It's a disingenuous copy of something else, it's inherently deceiving. Another example, a fake iPhone. A fake iPhone could have potentially dangerous components. It's made only to make money off the unsuspecting. 

Better words instead of ''fake'' could be: staged, choreographed, scripted, etc etc.

Calling it ''fake'' just makes you look like a spiteful dullard.

author: @maulcs
text:
"Actions have consequences" my ***, there are hardly any impactful choices in this game. And the camp mechanics are basically meaningless as you can literally do nothing and things keep chugging along with zero actual consequences.

3:37 is also a deception, implying random NPCs are affected by you killing their families (that would have been great, some kind of SoM Nemesis system). Everyone is meaningless cannon fodder, just like GTA - and store/main NPCs will even magically respawn, protecting you from the consequences of your actions.

It's a superb game in some aspects (graphics, environment, story), but very flawed in others (gameplay mechanics, including the horrendous mask/bounty system, and painfully linear missions).

author: @rg975
text: 4 years later and I'm still finding new things in this game. While it does suffer from some pacing issues (the opening act), poorly implemented honor system, and some odd design decisions, once you dig your heels in, it is heavily outweighed by the sheer content, quality, and care that was poured into the single player. There are so many things to do its hard to comprehend. I still have not played another open world game that can match the detail and scope of RDR2. It is the best game I've played.

text: I remember the time where I watched this trailer by chance in youtube at school and when I watched you can  hunt, eat, take a bath and etc. I was hyped even though I didn't watch the title of the vid. Turns out the game was releasing one week away, I was like yep pre ordering it. Then when I saw John, I was like is this the game where u shoot ur horse and skin it. I hardly remember coz it was 8 years ago and it's been so long since I played the first one. Then when October 26 hits and my pre load finished, I immediately grab my ps4 into my other room and play the game alone. My god I spent 15 hours that day playing the game, I also used remote play on my macbook to play this game. God, I wish I could play this game from the beginning again, Timeless classic.

text:
I think this game marked my life. When I started playing it :) and when I finished playing it :(
It's a beautiful game and it's in my top 1 of best games
I remember spending a lot of time doing all the side missions, easter eggs, camp conversations, legendary animals and other activities
I was even depressed for almost 1 month after finishing the game (I also played as much as possible with John)
And after almost 3 years I still remember and every time I want to play again, but I know that my heart will not take it again.
I may seem weak and I may be, but no game has made me feel like the protagonist and that is important in a game, to feel inside the game

Me along with others are asking that you fix the broken witness and wanted system.

I figured it bugged out I went to walk away he fires shots at me

Right as the gunshot sounds it instantly spawns to a bounty now I'm getting chased by the person that told on me 
Along with the relentless never ending spawning of lawman,

On top of a bounty...


so if the witness system was working correctly how can you stop a witness when they're also a hostile NCP,


this is just a tip of the iceberg of the multiple problems and bugs that are still in this game that you're still selling for $60..


So on behalf of all the paying customers can you fix the same exact documented problem that hey y'all did fix in online...

You don't even need to dig too deep into it just basically fix and allow story mode offline players to have the same benefit that you're giving people online you made the game and the system part is broken and you chose to fix it an online but you're choosing not to do it in offline and we need an answer.

author: @leek3513
text:
I find it fascinating that People don’t realize this game is about heaven and hell
& that Arthur and  Dutch represent the Ways of repent for god as once said
“A bad man who turns to god will me saved” | *Arthur*
“A good man who turns away from god will fail” | *Dutch*
Arthur was always bad as a kid and Dutch was the one who always influenced him to be a better man which is heard in multiple dialogue and eventually he did.
*Micah* Represents A *demon*
Slippery sneaky and a filthy man that always lies and sins 
And Micah showed us that demons can ruin anything built up over a period of time ( the gang) through 
•Manipulation: Always in his ear 
•Greed: money 
•Lust: Beautiful women in Tahiti money and freedom 
*Dutch* betrays everyone for *micah* because he was basically demon possession 
And when *Arthur* dies your reputation will show a heaven and hell
•Good: beautiful land and lots of light and deer represents Peace 
•Bad: dark land of rain and thunder the wolf represents he has preyed on everything sinful (resembled of the wolf eating the dead animal) and then followed up by him running into a endless pit of darkness 
This is telling us anyone can turn to and stray from our lord god. 
Always see the bigger picture 
At the end when *dutch* kills *micah* it shows you that by the time he had slayed his demon it was to late and everything was lost

author: @OntologicallySteve7765
text:
This game has had such an impact upon me. 

The fact that Arthur's dying wish was to face the West and watch one last sunset (to remember the nostalgia of the good ole days) is profoundly meaningful. It's chocked full of esoteric profundity. But, yet: it didn't quite turn out that way.

The sunset denotes the end----- a fast-approaching impending darkness and gloom-------a darkness that overcomes and chases out the light. It's a reflection of the past.

The sunrise denotes a new beginning-------the all-consuming influx of light...the dawn of a new day-----a day that penetrates and chases out the darkness. It's a reflection of how the past has ended-------and a new beginning has dawned. It's an acknowledgement of the past as a seed-----------and the future as the harvest of a life well lived (that transcends even in death).

On account of Arthur dying as the sun chased away the darkness: does this mean he didn't get what he wanted? No. Not at all. By an unseen force-----it seems his expectations were exceeded. There's such a rich philosophical-undertone to this. 

This is what's on John's grave-marker: "Blessed are the peacemakers"------[for they shall see the Kingdom of God}. 

The John Marston that we've all grown to love wouldn't be the man he was if not for Arthur. Arthur was the chisel that fashioned and formed John. Even in his death (per his epitaph): we see a bit of Arthur. Out of the chaos that Dutch's decisions perpetuated: Arthur bravely chose to use his final moments in a sacrificial way-----------thus creating a domino effect of peace and light . This order out of chaos was the butterfly effect that turned darkness into light, thus fashioning John into the self-sacrificing person that he would inevitably become.

For better or for worse: all of us are influencing someone. I know he's just a video game character------but Arthur Morgan has made me want to be a better man. In that, he's so much more than "just" a video game character. Arthur Morgan transcends the medium and has supplanted himself in the deepest recesses of my soul. That's no small accomplishment. Even if I never played the game again: Arthur Morgan will always be with me. His ghost is a welcome reminder that integrity and truth is all that matters. It's more important than life itself. 

This is the ideological-impression that the game has forever imprinted on me: Your life can be taken from you----------but death can't rob you of the truth-laden integrity that perpetuated the death. Sometimes, integrity equates to tragedy. Even still, look at Dutch's end...his lack of integrity produced tragedy (just the same). But it was a shameful death that offered no redemption.

We all gotta live for something. If you're not willing to die for something---------then you're not living for anything.  

Who'd of thought that RockStar Games would have the ability to influence me so deeply------even on the cellular level? It's a travesty that this game didn't win game of the year. This says more about the one's who made this decision than it ever will about RDR2. People nowadays are so morbidly-superficial and chronically shallow.

Anyhow, if all of us were effected by Arthur (as John was): it's no stretch to say that we'd be a better person for it. One day, we're all going to face our end. Will our end be accompanied by a sunrise or a sunset? The choice is yours. Yes, it may be "just a video game: but if you apply the lessons, it transcends the medium. If it remains "just a video game" it's because you failed to see the underlying implications that are beckoning you to become a better version of who you already are. Our decisions not only effect US: They effect everyone around us. 

In the dichotomy that exists between Dutch and Arthur, we're faced with the decision to either become a better version of who we already are---------or a bad one. In this RPG called life-----may we proceed with heartfelt caution. Every decision is the flick of a domino that pushes us toward the chain reaction of damnation--------or redemption.

author: @reddwarf3046
text: GTA5 & RDR2 are modernized takes on Super Mario Bros and Kart. A board (map) with progressive levels (story chapters) with power ups (weapon upgrades) and bonous worlds (extra tasks). First it was Atari's Pong. The first mainstream video game when video games were still like board games. The inventor of Super Mario Bros., Shigeru Miyamoto, changed video games forever when he joined Nintendo in 1977 to take them into video games. Sam & Dan Houser took Shigeru's format and brought it into the 3D world with GTA3, and again video games were brought to a new level. GTA5 and RDR2 brought the technical benchmark again to a new standard for all games. I'm interested to see what the next big revolutionary idea in gaming is...

author: @74santafe
text: After just 2 hours of gameplay, i can say this is overhyped (mind you i prefer Souls-like games, and nothing comes close to those so a comparison is a bit unfair for sure). Might get better later, but the first hours are typical "hold you by the hand" guided gameplay made for children perhaps. You can't really fail, no feeling of risk involved. Also  every single action like searching a drawer seems to take 5-10 seconds, and movement is sluggish as hell, feels like the controller has got some delay built into it (PS4 Pro). Beautiful graphics and sound though, and i hope things will change after some more playing. Then i will come back and say  i was wrong.

author: @deenanthekemoni9821
text: Im about eighteen hours into the story, and there Are several things I love and few I dislike. The gunplay isnt as sophisticated as I had hoped, but is still manageable. Hunting is excellent, feels challenging and stressful as it should be, animals certainly don't just let you kill them. If you buy it be patient, it takes awhile to really get going, you're not going to be open world mode for a few hours, but its necessary as there are so many mechanics to learn and so much info to take in. But once you get to it exploring feels appropriately dangerous, as it would have been in 1899. Best graphics I have ever seen, in any game on console, in my entire life. This will be GOTY, go get yourself a copy and enjoy the experience.

GTA 5: 
Gunplay-Trash
Driving- Trash

We want REALISM. We want it to actually feel like we sent a slug of metal through the air and into/through flesh. 

We want bullet travel/drop physics. Not COD’s arcade point and shoot garbage like GTA V.

The driving and shooting we’re so easy in GTA V that I was bored with it very quickly. 

Mostly I am just sad and disappointed that Rockstar thinks they have to water down their “realism”. JUST PLAIN SAD

author: @Aaror1000
text: If you watch from 4:03 in slow motion you can really see how much effort the animators put into this game. The gun shown here is a Browning Auto-5. It is somewhat unique because in real life the entire barrel reciprocates with the bolt when fired. I was amazed to see that the animators had actually included this interesting mechanical quirk into the game even though most people would never even notice.

author: @og9575
text: I feel like no matter how much any game tries to let you decide what kind of character the player-character has, it's mainly dictated by the narrative and what happens in cutscenes when you don't have control over what Arthur says and does then. Characters always have backstories and past actions that you are meant to make inferences on that, when you get down to it, really paints who they are and what would make sense for them to do. It would be like playing a Tell-Tale Batman game as the Joker and making Joker jump in front of a bullet to save someone. It just wouldn't make sense. Maybe the Joker reference would in some layered sense, but you get the point.

author: @irecordwithaphone1856
text:
I think I’m gonna play beginning as a hardcore outlaw that if I witness a crime I’ll keep walking, but throughout Arthur’s story I’ll start helping people out more and roleplaying as if he’s discovering what the limit to corruption and bad deeds should be for himself, I’ll start helping strangers and maybe stopping robberies based on context, if it’s a robbery of a store in a small town where the shopkeeper makes less money than a big city shopkeeper I’ll stop the robbery, but if it’s in a big city I’ll let it be unless things get out of control. Mixing my own crime with being a decent person to the poorer and more common folk. 

For a real redemption story I want Arthur to go from stone cold to a bit more understanding of others and the world. He still loves his ‘family’ but won’t let them get in the way of his morals later in the story.

text:
I just realized that the great thing about them Lying about delaying it on the PC until they sell enough Console copies, is that by the time the game comes out on the PC the graphics engine will be outdated. It will still look and run a thousand times better on PC. But 1 to 1.1/2 years is a long time in gaming.

Also a reminder that there is literally no reason to not release it on PC the same day. There is no technical or physical limitation keeping them from releasing it on PC. GTAV made literally all the money in the world. They could afford to have a perfect crystal clear flawless PC port on day 1 a thousand times over. But since it worked so well with GTAV. The only reason not to release it at the same time for PC is to get people to buy it twice.

author: @voltshock99
text: Yeah, Rockstar takes a long time between games, but that's why they're always at such high quality. No game is perfect, but Rockstar puts lots of care and effort into their products. A lot of franchises that make new sequels every year tend to cut corners by recycling assets, limiting effects and details, or just otherwise not fleshing out games as fully as they could be. As much as I loved Farcry 5, I knew it could have been so much better if the developers had just been given a little more time. That's why I appreciate Rockstar: in an industry seemingly run by fat cats whose sole concern is squeezing as much money as possible from the wallets of players, it's nice to know that at least one company seems to care more about making a good game than making a quick buck.

author: @NightIceWolf12
text: When I play a game I like to do all the side quests, explore, and the like. However, the video said that the camp will be changing locations frequently and in previous videos or interviews there were talk that the outlaws might get testy with Arthur if he is exploring all day and not helping the camp, such as hunting for food. Can I play the game how I want to and not miss too much side content. I understand that if I explore all day then I might not get side missions from the outlaws since they will be mad that I haven't been helping them. However, will I be able to go back to previous areas of the map or have to stay in an area around the camp and the game will be in sections? I am getting worried that I won't be able to explore and do side content all that much since I will have to worry about the outlaws not giving me side content since they are angry at me. Also, can the player control when the outlaws changes location? So, if I want to do a whole bunch of content in let's say the swamp area then will the camp just leave me or do I have to do a main mission in that area to activate the change locations function (running away from a town that you robbed their bank), or do I have to go up to someone and say that it is about time to change locations? author: @desync4058
text:
Game ideas: 
#1 ( Online ) Gangs:
This should be straight forward, online communities wether a player wants to set a gang for themselves, or players joining a " gang " . They can set a camp ( and/or steal/build a town or hub )  for up to 50 membera or more.

#2 Trading:
An open world with players around every corner calls chances everywhere to be made. You can communicate with other gangs ( players ) to make a trade. You can trade your money for a horse and stuff. ( IF you wanna pay for it. Nobody said everything is for free... But nobody told you to play along nicely. ) You can call gang members along if you feel like a betrayal will follow.

#3 Bounty hunting: If a gang member of yours or others are being a rebel, towns folk will report to the cops and set a bounty on that player. This sets opportunities for OTHER gang members ( world wide ) to set out and target that player. This can go 3 ways... 1, YOUR gang members will shoot back at the hunter. 2, your OWN Gang member will shoot you in the back for the cash. 3, Fakely bring in your gang member, get paid, and break the member out of jail. 

Guys, please report these ideas for RockStar. This game has a LOT of potential.

author: @jordanwolfson8378
text:
Call me a simpleton, i don't care. This seems like too much for me. I'm all one for immersion and stuff, but there's a line where immersion turns into a chore. I don't want to have to wash my horse daily, or else it will start hating me and won't let me ride it. I want to ride around the landscape and experience it, without so many chores and grind-y duties. I want to shoot a gun and clear out a room of baddies, not have to wait fifteen seconds for a reload and possibly get killed in the process.

Also, who else wants to bet horses will cost real life money? A Clydesdale will probably cost 20 real life dollars. No thank you!

JOHN MARSTON AND HIS FAMILY AT 2:34 
John and Abigail had two children together, John Marston Jr. (a.k.a. "Jack"), and an unnamed daughter, who passed away sometime before 1911. Jack grew up amongst the gang, oftentimes referring to Dutch, Bill, and possibly Javier as uncles. The daughter's name is never told but when John was talking about his family, he said his daughter was taken by cholera.

author: @victorko9627
text:
Dutch: Hello again, John.
Marston: Hello, Dutch.
Dutch: We gotta stop meeting like this!
Marston: Sure!
Dutch: I've got a plan, John.
Marston: You've always got a plan, Dutch.
Dutch: This is a good one.
[He throws down his gun.]
Marston: I don't doubt it.
Dutch: We can't always fight nature, John. We can't fight change. We can't fight gravity. We can't fight nothing. My whole life, all I ever did was fight.
Marston: Then give up, Dutch.
Dutch: But I can't give up, neither. I can't fight my own nature. That's the paradox, John. You see?
Marston: Then I have to shoot you.
Dutch: When I'm gone, they'll just find another monster. They have to, because they have to justify their wages.
Marston: That's their business.
Dutch: Our time is passed, John.


EPIC



CANT WAIT TO KNOW HOW IT ALL BEGAN
